textPlain:
Back in the internet's early days, it was easy to get a domain name. They were cheap or even free.

That is not exactly how I remember things. Everyone bought domains from one registrar (was it Network Solutions?) and the cheapest domain started at around $100. There were all sorts of short and trademarked dot-coms available though, that is true.I'm going by ancient memory here and would love it if someone corrects me.

If he’s sitting on a domain that’s actually worth millions and has a well-paying job, maybe he’d get a bite for “I’ll accept less than 10 if you match it 1:1 with a donation to <charity>.”It’s his and he can do what he wants. In fact I think it’s cool that there’s still a Classic Web holdout like this. But there’s latent joy locked away in that url.
I believe this was one of, if not the first site to implement a barcode/UPC generator.  Current iteration here:https://milk.com/barcode/

purple.com was a similar thing.  Originally the site had just a FAQ page about how they didn't want to sell the domain:https://web.archive.org/web/20090226000820/http://www.purple...https://web.archive.org/web/20090225184613/http://www.purple...I just checked now and it's one of those mattress drop-shipping sites :-(
In a weird way, when someone mentions something about milk with a domain name, it always remind me of the Milk Table.https://milk.dk
This guy worked at Danger! Man I loved my Hiptop/Sidekick

I found out about milk.com when I was thinking about how to make an Android app completely from scratch (assembling DEX bytes from zero, kind of like writing an assembler for the Dalvik VM). That’s when I came across the author of the DEX format, Dan Bornstein — and I was surprised he actually owns a domain like that.
https://milk.com/value/I think he’s aiming a bit high with 10 milly.  I’d take 1 or 5.

Plus you could only interact with NS by filling in fields in a text file and emailing it to them, and hoping you did it right so their script made the changes you intended.  Even at the time, this was annoying.
There are a lot of people that saw domain names as real estate in the 90s. :D
I use http://neverssl.com as its replacement when checking internet connectivity.
I don't dislike the design, but this (500€!) aquarium is criminally small, and there's two fish in there!https://milk.dk/wp-content/uploads/2021/09/Milk_aquarium-102...
Not to be confused with a milking table. (Not a term I'd recommend googling at work, by the way.)

If the milk marketing budget is truly 100 million a year (a cursory check shows it's likely closer to 200 million now) as he mentions, 10 million doesn't seem very high at all.So they've had a 100 million per year marketing budget for at least 10 years. Let's say they spent 1% per year towards the domain... The opportunity cost for not having something as obvious and useful as milk.com seems significant. To me it seems like they've already made a mistake by not paying up despite having the means, and now they've dug their foot in. They've spent hundreds of millions on billboards, yet they can't even put "milk.com" on their billboards next to "got milk". Imo but 5% less billboards for a few years and buy the domain...
I'd love to have a job that I liked so much I'd keep doing it rather than cash out a domain name and retire.
